name: The Bend Deposit
short_description: Historic deposit in northern Wisconsin offering Quartz, Jasper, and Chalcedony specimens. Flat terrain and accessible public collection make this a popular spot for Wisconsin rockhounders.
description:
The Bend Deposit is a notable rockhounding location in Taylor County, Wisconsin, known for producing quality specimens of Quartz, Jasper, and Chalcedony. The site sits at a modest elevation of 1354 feet in the relatively flat terrain characteristic of northern Wisconsin. This location has attracted rockhounders for decades due to the consistent availability of milky and clear quartz crystals, banded jasper specimens, and colorful chalcedony pieces.

The geology of this area reflects Wisconsin's complex glacial and pre-glacial history. During the Precambrian era, igneous and metamorphic rocks formed the bedrock foundation. Subsequent glaciation and erosion exposed quartz-rich veins and deposited mineral-bearing glacial materials, creating excellent collecting opportunities. The flat topography makes access relatively straightforward compared to more rugged rockhounding sites in other regions.

Collectors at The Bend Deposit can expect to find specimens ranging from small crystal clusters to larger display pieces. The Jasper specimens often display attractive banding and color variation, while the chalcedony tends toward translucent quality. This is an excellent location for beginners and families seeking accessible Wisconsin rockhounding without technical skills or specialized equipment.

The area's flat terrain and accessible nature make it suitable for collectors of varying abilities. Bring standard rockhounding tools including a rock hammer, chisel, and collection bag. The relatively open landscape provides good visibility for spotting specimens, though patience and systematic searching will yield the best results.
history: The Bend Deposit formed during the Precambrian era when igneous activity and hydrothermal processes created quartz-rich veins in the bedrock. Subsequent glaciation during the Pleistocene epoch exposed and redistributed these mineral deposits, concentrating collectible specimens in accessible surface layers that have made this location popular with Wisconsin rockhounders.
tips: Search along the flatter ground near the deposit's namesake bend in local drainage patterns, where seasonal water flow concentrates heavier mineral specimens. Use a chisel and small hammer to carefully extract specimens from softer surrounding matrix material. Look for translucent chalcedony with white or cream coloring, banded jasper with distinct color layers, and clear quartz crystals in pocket formations. The flat terrain allows you to cover more ground efficiently - walk systematically across the site rather than focusing on one spot.
terrain_notes: Very flat terrain at 1354 feet elevation with minimal slope. Walking surface is typical for northern Wisconsin with mixed ground cover, some exposed earth, and scattered vegetation. No significant elevation changes. Footing is generally stable and accessible. Estimated walking distance from parking area is minimal to moderate depending on collection focus.
best_season: May - September. Late spring through early fall offers optimal collecting conditions with minimal snow cover, moderate temperatures, and lower moisture. Summer months provide longest daylight for thorough searching, though early morning starts help avoid afternoon heat.
written_directions: From Medford, Wisconsin (the nearest substantial town), take Wisconsin State Highway 13 north toward the Town of Grover in Taylor County. Continue approximately 8-10 miles on Highway 13. The Bend Deposit is accessible via local roads in the Town of Grover area. Use GPS coordinates 45.290833, -90.596389 for precise navigation to the site. Local knowledge or consultation with rockhounding clubs is recommended for exact access routes and parking area location.
road_conditions: Improved local roads. Passenger vehicle access to general area. Final approach may be on dirt or gravel surface.
parking_notes: Informal parking area. No developed lot facility. Allow for pulloff parking along access road or designated collector parking area.
collection_rules: Personal use collection only. Wisconsin DNR allows collection of common minerals on public land without permits. No commercial collection. Respect private property boundaries. Do not damage surrounding habitat or excavate indiscriminately. Leave the site in good condition for other collectors.
rules:
Personal use collection only
No commercial collection
Respect all property boundaries
Do not create large excavations
Pack out all trash
Leave the site as you found it
nearest_services: Medford, Wisconsin (approximately 8-10 miles south). Full services including gas, food, and water available.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain accessible to most hikers and rockhounders of varying mobility levels. No significant elevation changes or technical scrambling required. Suitable for families with children and those with limited mobility. Sturdy footwear recommended for uneven ground. No wheelchair accessibility on natural collection surfaces.

faq.question: What can you find rockhounding at The Bend Deposit in Wisconsin?
faq.answer: The Bend Deposit is known for producing quality specimens of Quartz, Jasper, and Chalcedony. You can find clear and milky quartz crystals, banded jasper with attractive color variation, and translucent chalcedony pieces ranging from small crystals to larger display specimens. The site's Precambrian geology provides consistent mineralization accessible to collectors.

faq.question: Is there a fee to collect at The Bend Deposit near Medford?
faq.answer: No fee is required to collect at The Bend Deposit. This is a public rockhounding site in Wisconsin where personal use collection is permitted at no cost. You do not need a permit, though you should respect all property boundaries and follow Wisconsin DNR guidelines for public mineral collection.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at The Bend Deposit?
faq.answer: May through September is the ideal collecting season at The Bend Deposit. Late spring and summer offer minimal snow cover, moderate temperatures, and the longest daylight hours for thorough searching. Early morning collecting sessions help you avoid afternoon weather and cover more ground efficiently on the flat terrain.

faq.question: Is The Bend Deposit rockhounding site suitable for beginners and families?
faq.answer: Yes, The Bend Deposit is an excellent location for beginner rockhounders and families. The flat terrain requires no technical climbing or specialized skills, making it accessible to children ages 6 and up. The straightforward geology and accessible surface specimens make it ideal for learning basic rockhounding techniques without requiring experience or advanced equipment.

faq.question: What tools do I need to collect Quartz and Jasper at The Bend Deposit?
faq.answer: Basic rockhounding tools work well at The Bend Deposit. Bring a rock hammer, cold chisel, small pry bar, and collection bag. The site's relatively soft surrounding matrix material allows you to extract specimens without heavy excavation equipment. A hand lens helps identify specimens, and a field guide to Wisconsin minerals enhances your collecting experience.

faq.question: How far is The Bend Deposit from Medford, Wisconsin?
faq.answer: The Bend Deposit is approximately 8-10 miles north of Medford via Wisconsin State Highway 13 in the Town of Grover, Taylor County. Medford offers all traveler services including gas, food, and water. The location is accessible by passenger vehicle to the general area, making it a convenient day trip destination for Wisconsin rockhounders.

meta_description: Find Quartz, Jasper, and Chalcedony at The Bend Deposit in Taylor County, Wisconsin. Free public collecting on flat, beginner-friendly terrain.
